vulnerabilities.message: Dynamic script loading without integrity verification - vulnerable to supply chain attacks
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add Subresource Integrity (SRI) hash to verify script integrity: script.integrity = "sha384-..."

attackVector.description: Loading external scripts without integrity verification allows attackers to inject malicious code if the CDN is compromised or the connection is intercepted.
attackVector.exploitExample: const script = document.createElement("script"); script.src = "https://cdn.com/lib.js"; → CDN compromise injects malicious code
realWorldImpact: Supply chain attacks via CDN compromise
Remote code execution from tampered scripts

remediation.explanation: Always verify integrity of dynamically loaded scripts using SRI hashes to prevent supply chain attacks.

vulnerabilities.message: Prototype pollution vulnerability detected
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Validate object keys before assignment, use Object.create(null), or use Map instead
vulnerabilities.owasp: A08:2025 - Software and Data Integrity Failures

attackVector.description: Prototype pollution allows attackers to inject properties into Object.prototype, affecting all objects and potentially leading to privilege escalation or code execution.
attackVector.exploitExample: obj[key] = value where key = "__proto__" injects properties into all objects

remediation.before: const obj = {}; obj[userKey] = userValue; // If userKey = "__proto__", pollutes all objects
remediation.explanation: Always validate object keys before assignment. Use Object.create(null) for objects without prototype, or use Map instead

vulnerabilities.triageReason: CVSS 7.5 (high) + Software and Data Integrity Failures + production code

vulnerabilities.message: AI-generated code detected (HIGH confidence): 11 hallucinated method(s) found (.remove, .contains)
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Replace hallucinated methods with correct JavaScript equivalents. Found: .remove(), .contains(). Review and rewrite AI-generated code sections.

attackVector.description: AI-generated code with hallucinated methods creates runtime TypeError exceptions exposing system internals through stack traces. Attackers can trigger these errors repeatedly to map application structure and identify vulnerable endpoints. Inconsistent code patterns make security reviews difficult, allowing real vulnerabilities to hide among AI-generated noise. Over-engineered error handling may leak sensitive information in catch blocks.
attackVector.exploitExample:
User triggers: array.append(item)
Result: TypeError: array.append is not a function
Stack trace reveals: Internal file paths, function names, database schema hints
Attacker uses this to map application architecture and plan targeted attacks.
realWorldImpact: Runtime errors revealing sensitive stack traces with internal paths
Logic bugs in access control or validation code (AI-generated if statements)
Performance degradation from inefficient AI-generated loops and algorithms
Maintenance burden: Developers spend hours debugging AI hallucinations
Hidden security vulnerabilities masked by over-engineered code patterns

remediation.after: array.splice() or .filter()  // Correct JavaScript
remediation.explanation: AI code generators (like GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT, Claude) can hallucinate non-existent methods or generate over-engineered patterns. This creates reliability issues and potential security vulnerabilities. Hallucinated methods cause runtime errors that expose stack traces with sensitive information. Over-engineered code patterns make security audits difficult, hiding real vulnerabilities. Always verify AI-generated code matches language specifications and follows security best practices.

vulnerabilities.message: XSS: innerHTML with unsanitized user content
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use textContent, DOMPurify.sanitize(), or createElement()

attackVector.description: An attacker can inject malicious JavaScript code through user input, stealing session cookies, credentials, or performing actions on behalf of the user.
attackVector.exploitExample: element.innerHTML = userInput where userInput = ""

remediation.after:
element.textContent = userContent; // Safe for plain text
// Or: element.innerHTML = DOMPurify.sanitize(userContent);
remediation.explanation: Use textContent for plain text, or sanitize HTML with DOMPurify before setting innerHTML

vulnerabilities.message: XSS vulnerability in HTML template literal
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Sanitize variables or use React's JSX (auto-escaped)

attackVector.description: Template literals with user input in SQL queries, commands, eval, or HTML contexts allow attackers to inject malicious code, leading to XSS, SQL injection, or command execution.
attackVector.exploitExample: `Link` allows XSS if userInput contains javascript:alert(1)

remediation.explanation: When rendering HTML with template literals, all variables must be sanitized. Use DOMPurify or avoid template literals for HTML entirely.

vulnerabilities.message: Promise without catch handler may cause unhandled rejections
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add .catch() handler or use try-catch with async/await

attackVector.description: Unhandled promise rejections can crash Node.js applications and expose error details
attackVector.exploitExample: fetch("/api/data").then(data => process(data)); // no error handling
realWorldImpact: Service unavailability and denial of service

remediation.after: fetch("/api/data").then(data => process(data)).catch(err => logger.error("Request failed"));
remediation.explanation: Unhandled promise rejections can crash applications and expose sensitive debugging information

vulnerabilities.message: setTimeout/setInterval with string or code variable executes code like eval()
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use function reference or arrow function instead

attackVector.description: Passing a string or variable containing code to setTimeout/setInterval causes it to be evaluated as code, similar to eval().
attackVector.exploitExample: setTimeout("alert(userInput)", 1000) or setTimeout(code, 1000) where code/userInput is attacker-controlled

remediation.after: setTimeout(() => doSomething(), 1000);
remediation.explanation: Always pass a function reference or arrow function to setTimeout/setInterval, never a string or variable containing code

vulnerabilities.message: Hardcoded credentials in dictionary/config object
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use environment variables with os.getenv() or python-dotenv

attackVector.description: Hardcoded credentials in configuration dictionaries are visible to anyone with repository access and persist in version control history forever.
attackVector.exploitExample: "password": "SuperSecret123!" # Visible in Git history, cannot be rotated

remediation.after:
import os
DATABASE_CONFIG = {"password": os.getenv("DB_PASSWORD")}  # Store in .env file
remediation.explanation: Store secrets in environment variables. Use python-dotenv for local development, AWS Secrets Manager or similar for production

vulnerabilities.triageReason: CVSS 9.1 (critical) + Identification and Authentication Failures + production code

vulnerabilities.message: print() can leak sensitive information
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use logging module with appropriate levels
vulnerabilities.owasp: A09:2025 - Security Logging and Monitoring Failures

attackVector.description: print() statements can expose sensitive data in logs, stack traces, or console output accessible to unauthorized users.
attackVector.exploitExample: print(f"User logged in: {user.email}, token: {auth_token}")  # Exposes credentials

remediation.after:
import logging
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
logger.info(f"User logged in: {user.email}")  # No sensitive data
remediation.explanation: Use logging module with appropriate levels. Never log sensitive data (tokens, passwords, keys)

vulnerabilities.triageReason: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures + production code

vulnerabilities.message: Path Traversal risk in os.path.join() with variable 'filename'
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Validate filename does not contain ".." before using os.path.join()

attackVector.description: os.path.join() does not prevent path traversal. If 'filename' contains "../" sequences, attackers can escape the base directory and access arbitrary files.
attackVector.exploitExample: os.path.join('/var/uploads/', filename) where filename = '../../../etc/passwd' results in '/var/uploads/../../../etc/passwd'

remediation.after:
from pathlib import Path

# Validate filename doesn't contain path traversal
if '..' in filename or filename.startsWith('/'):
    raise ValueError("Invalid filename")

# Use pathlib for safer path construction
BASE_DIR = Path('/var/uploads')
safe_path = (BASE_DIR / filename).resolve()

if not safe_path.is_relative_to(BASE_DIR):
    raise ValueError("Path traversal detected")

with open(safe_path) as f:
remediation.explanation: Always validate that filenames do not contain ".." before using os.path.join(). Use pathlib.Path.resolve() and is_relative_to() for robust path validation

vulnerabilities.message: Insecure deserialization with pickle - allows arbitrary code execution when deserializing untrusted data
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use safe serialization formats (JSON, MessagePack) or implement strict validation before pickle deserialization

attackVector.description: Pickle can execute arbitrary Python code during deserialization. If an attacker controls the serialized data, they can achieve Remote Code Execution (RCE), install backdoors, steal credentials, or completely compromise the server. This is one of the most critical Python vulnerabilities.
attackVector.exploitExample:
import pickle
data = pickle.loads(request.data)  # CRITICAL: RCE vulnerability!
realWorldImpact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) when deserializing attacker-controlled data
Complete server compromise and backdoor installation

remediation.explanation: Never use pickle for untrusted data. Prefer JSON (json.loads), MessagePack (msgpack.unpackb), or Protocol Buffers. If pickle is absolutely required, implement HMAC signature verification, use restricted Unpickler with safe_load(), and validate all data in isolated sandbox.

vulnerabilities.message: Path Traversal risk in os.path.join() with variable 'file_name'
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Validate filename does not contain ".." before using os.path.join()

attackVector.description: os.path.join() does not prevent path traversal. If 'file_name' contains "../" sequences, attackers can escape the base directory and access arbitrary files.
attackVector.exploitExample: os.path.join('/var/uploads/', filename) where filename = '../../../etc/passwd' results in '/var/uploads/../../../etc/passwd'

remediation.after:
from pathlib import Path

# Validate filename doesn't contain path traversal
if '..' in file_name or file_name.startsWith('/'):
    raise ValueError("Invalid filename")

# Use pathlib for safer path construction
BASE_DIR = Path('/var/uploads')
safe_path = (BASE_DIR / file_name).resolve()

if not safe_path.is_relative_to(BASE_DIR):
    raise ValueError("Path traversal detected")

with open(safe_path) as f:
remediation.explanation: Always validate that filenames do not contain ".." before using os.path.join(). Use pathlib.Path.resolve() and is_relative_to() for robust path validation

attackVector.description: Improper resource cleanup during exceptions can lead to resource leaks and denial of service
attackVector.exploitExample: file = open("sensitive.txt"); data = file.read() # no finally or with statement
realWorldImpact: Memory leaks from unclosed file handles
Denial of service from resource exhaustion

remediation.after:
with open("data.txt") as file:
    # operations here
remediation.explanation: Resources opened without guaranteed cleanup can cause leaks when exceptions occur

vulnerabilities.message: Missing await on potentially async function call
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add await keyword before async function calls

attackVector.description: Calling an async function without await causes the function to return a coroutine object instead of the expected result, leading to silent failures where code appears to run but does nothing.
attackVector.exploitExample: result = fetch_user_data()  # Returns coroutine, not user data

remediation.after: user = await get_user_by_id(user_id)
remediation.explanation: Always use await with async functions - missing await causes silent failures

vulnerabilities.message: input() without validation can accept malicious data
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Validate and sanitize all user input with type conversion or regex

attackVector.description: Python's input() accepts any string, which can be manipulated to cause issues if not validated (especially in scripts with system access).
attackVector.exploitExample: filename = input("Enter filename: ")  # User enters "../../etc/passwd"
realWorldImpact: Command injection if passed to shell
Denial of Service with large inputs

remediation.explanation: Always validate and convert user input. Use int()/float() for numbers, regex for strings, and whitelist validation

vulnerabilities.message: AI-generated code detected (MEDIUM confidence): 1 hallucinated method(s) found (.add)
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Replace hallucinated methods with correct Python equivalents. Found: .add(). Review and rewrite AI-generated code sections.

attackVector.description: AI-generated code with hallucinated methods creates runtime AttributeError exceptions exposing system internals through stack traces. Attackers can trigger these errors repeatedly to map application structure and identify vulnerable endpoints. Inconsistent code patterns (mixing camelCase and snake_case) make security reviews difficult, allowing real vulnerabilities to hide among AI-generated noise. Over-engineered error handling may leak sensitive information in except blocks.
attackVector.exploitExample:
User triggers: items.push(value)
Result: AttributeError: 'list' object has no attribute 'push'
Stack trace reveals: Internal file paths, function names, database schema hints
Attacker uses this to map application architecture and plan targeted attacks.
realWorldImpact: Runtime errors revealing sensitive stack traces with internal paths
Logic bugs in access control or validation code (AI-generated if statements)
Performance degradation from inefficient AI-generated loops and algorithms
Maintenance burden: Developers spend hours debugging AI hallucinations
Hidden security vulnerabilities masked by over-engineered code patterns

remediation.after: items.append() for lists  # Correct Python
remediation.explanation: AI code generators (like GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT, Claude) can hallucinate non-existent methods or generate over-engineered patterns. This creates reliability issues and potential security vulnerabilities. Hallucinated methods cause AttributeError exceptions that expose stack traces with sensitive information. Over-engineered code patterns make security audits difficult, hiding real vulnerabilities. Always verify AI-generated code matches Python specifications (PEP 8) and follows security best practices.

    raise ValueError("Path traversal detected")

with open(safe_path) as f:
remediation.explanation: Always validate that filenames do not contain ".." before using os.path.join(). Use pathlib.Path.resolve() and is_relative_to() for robust path validation

attackVector.description: os.path.join() does not prevent path traversal. If 'filename' contains "../" sequences, attackers can escape the base directory and access arbitrary files.
attackVector.exploitExample: os.path.join('/var/uploads/', filename) where filename = '../../../etc/passwd' results in '/var/uploads/../../../etc/passwd'

remediation.after:
from pathlib import Path

# Validate filename doesn't contain path traversal
if '..' in filename or filename.startsWith('/'):
    raise ValueError("Invalid filename")

# Use pathlib for safer path construction
BASE_DIR = Path('/var/uploads')
safe_path = (BASE_DIR / filename).resolve()

if not safe_path.is_relative_to(BASE_DIR):
    raise ValueError("Path traversal detected")

with open(safe_path) as f:
remediation.explanation: Always validate that filenames do not contain ".." before using os.path.join(). Use pathlib.Path.resolve() and is_relative_to() for robust path validation

vulnerabilities.message: Sensitive data logged in plaintext - exposes credentials and secrets in log files
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Never log passwords, tokens, API keys, or other sensitive data. Log only non-sensitive identifiers

attackVector.description: Sensitive data in logs can be exposed through log aggregation systems, backup files, or compromised logging servers, leading to credential theft and unauthorized access
attackVector.exploitExample: logger.info(f"User logged in with password: {password}")  # Password exposed in logs!
realWorldImpact: API key/token theft from logging systems
Secrets leaked through log aggregation tools

remediation.after: logger.info(f"Login attempt for user: {username}")  # Only log username, not password
remediation.explanation: Never log passwords, tokens, API keys, credit cards, or other secrets. Log only non-sensitive identifiers like username, user ID, or transaction ID. Sanitize logs before storage.

vulnerabilities.message: SQL Injection with string interpolation detected
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use parameterized queries with placeholders

attackVector.description: String interpolation or concatenation in SQL queries allows attackers to inject malicious SQL code, bypassing authentication and accessing the entire database.
attackVector.exploitExample: cursor.execute(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}") where user_id = "1 OR 1=1"

remediation.after:
cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?", (user_id,))
# Or: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s", (user_id,))
remediation.explanation: Always use parameterized queries where user input is passed as separate parameters, never interpolated into the query string

vulnerabilities.message: __import__() allows arbitrary module loading

remediation.explanation: Never use __import__() with untrusted input. Use importlib.import_module() with strict whitelist validation

vulnerabilities.message: Insecure random number generator used - predictable for security purposes
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use secrets module (secrets.token_hex(), secrets.token_urlsafe()) or os.urandom() for cryptographic randomness

attackVector.description: Python's random module uses Mersenne Twister which is not cryptographically secure. Attackers can predict future outputs after observing a sequence of values, allowing session hijacking, token forgery, and password prediction
attackVector.exploitExample:
import random
token = str(random.random())  # Predictable! Can be guessed

remediation.before:
import random
token = str(random.random())
session_id = random.randint(1000, 9999)
remediation.after:
import secrets
token = secrets.token_hex(32)
session_id = secrets.randbelow(9000) + 1000
remediation.explanation: Never use random module for security. Use secrets module for tokens, passwords, and security-critical random values. The secrets module uses os.urandom() which is cryptographically secure.

vulnerabilities.message: Path Traversal risk in os.path.join() with variable 'file'
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Validate filename does not contain ".." before using os.path.join()

attackVector.description: os.path.join() does not prevent path traversal. If 'file' contains "../" sequences, attackers can escape the base directory and access arbitrary files.
attackVector.exploitExample: os.path.join('/var/uploads/', filename) where filename = '../../../etc/passwd' results in '/var/uploads/../../../etc/passwd'

remediation.after:
from pathlib import Path

# Validate filename doesn't contain path traversal
if '..' in file or file.startsWith('/'):
    raise ValueError("Invalid filename")

# Use pathlib for safer path construction
BASE_DIR = Path('/var/uploads')
safe_path = (BASE_DIR / file).resolve()

if not safe_path.is_relative_to(BASE_DIR):
    raise ValueError("Path traversal detected")

with open(safe_path) as f:
remediation.explanation: Always validate that filenames do not contain ".." before using os.path.join(). Use pathlib.Path.resolve() and is_relative_to() for robust path validation

vulnerabilities.triageReason: CVSS 7.5 (high) + Broken Access Control + production code

vulnerabilities.message: SQL Injection via unsafe variable 'query' (constructed on line 149)

attackVector.description: Variable 'query' contains SQL query with string interpolation (line 149), then passed to execute() without parameterization. This allows SQL injection attacks.
attackVector.exploitExample:
query = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}"  # Line 149
cursor.execute(query)  # Line 152 - Vulnerable!

remediation.before:
# Line 149:
query = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}"
# Line 152:
cursor.execute(query)
remediation.after:
cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?", (user_id,))
# Or: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s", (user_id,))
remediation.explanation: Always use parameterized queries where user input is passed as separate parameters, never interpolated into the query string

vulnerabilities.message: Resource may not be properly cleaned up in exception scenarios

with open("data.txt") as file:
    # operations here
remediation.explanation: Resources opened without guaranteed cleanup can cause leaks when exceptions occur

vulnerabilities.message: input() without validation can accept malicious data
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Validate and sanitize all user input with type conversion or regex

attackVector.description: Python's input() accepts any string, which can be manipulated to cause issues if not validated (especially in scripts with system access).
attackVector.exploitExample: filename = input("Enter filename: ")  # User enters "../../etc/passwd"
realWorldImpact: Command injection if passed to shell
Denial of Service with large inputs

remediation.explanation: Always validate and convert user input. Use int()/float() for numbers, regex for strings, and whitelist validation

vulnerabilities.message: Missing await on potentially async function call
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add await keyword before async function calls

attackVector.description: Calling an async function without await causes the function to return a coroutine object instead of the expected result, leading to silent failures where code appears to run but does nothing.
attackVector.exploitExample: result = fetch_user_data()  # Returns coroutine, not user data

remediation.after: user = await get_user_by_id(user_id)
remediation.explanation: Always use await with async functions - missing await causes silent failures

vulnerabilities.message: Hardcoded secret detected: Generic Private Key - ----...----
vulnerabilities.suggestion:
Generic private key detected in code

Recommended fix:
1. Move secret to environment variable
2. Load from secure vault (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault)
3. Use: os.environ.get("API_KEY")
4. Add to .gitignore if stored in config file
5. Rotate the exposed secret immediately

attackVector.description: Hardcoded generic private key exposed in source code. Visible to anyone with repository access.
attackVector.exploitExample: Attacker with code access can extract: ----...----

remediation.after:
import os
GENERIC_PRIVATE_KEY = os.environ.get("GENERIC_PRIVATE_KEY")  # Store in .env file
remediation.explanation:
Generic private key detected in code

Recommended fix:
1. Move secret to environment variable
2. Load from secure vault (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault)
3. Use: os.environ.get("API_KEY")
4. Add to .gitignore if stored in config file
5. Rotate the exposed secret immediately

vulnerabilities.message: AI-generated code detected (MEDIUM confidence): 1 hallucinated method(s) found (.add)
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Replace hallucinated methods with correct Python equivalents. Found: .add(). Review and rewrite AI-generated code sections.

attackVector.description: AI-generated code with hallucinated methods creates runtime AttributeError exceptions exposing system internals through stack traces. Attackers can trigger these errors repeatedly to map application structure and identify vulnerable endpoints. Inconsistent code patterns (mixing camelCase and snake_case) make security reviews difficult, allowing real vulnerabilities to hide among AI-generated noise. Over-engineered error handling may leak sensitive information in except blocks.
attackVector.exploitExample:
User triggers: items.push(value)
Result: AttributeError: 'list' object has no attribute 'push'
Stack trace reveals: Internal file paths, function names, database schema hints
Attacker uses this to map application architecture and plan targeted attacks.
realWorldImpact: Runtime errors revealing sensitive stack traces with internal paths
Logic bugs in access control or validation code (AI-generated if statements)
Performance degradation from inefficient AI-generated loops and algorithms
Maintenance burden: Developers spend hours debugging AI hallucinations
Hidden security vulnerabilities masked by over-engineered code patterns

remediation.after: items.append() for lists  # Correct Python
remediation.explanation: AI code generators (like GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT, Claude) can hallucinate non-existent methods or generate over-engineered patterns. This creates reliability issues and potential security vulnerabilities. Hallucinated methods cause AttributeError exceptions that expose stack traces with sensitive information. Over-engineered code patterns make security audits difficult, hiding real vulnerabilities. Always verify AI-generated code matches Python specifications (PEP 8) and follows security best practices.

vulnerabilities.message: Sensitive data logged in plaintext - exposes credentials and secrets in log files
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Never log passwords, tokens, API keys, or other sensitive data. Log only non-sensitive identifiers

attackVector.description: Sensitive data in logs can be exposed through log aggregation systems, backup files, or compromised logging servers, leading to credential theft and unauthorized access
attackVector.exploitExample: logger.info(f"User logged in with password: {password}")  # Password exposed in logs!
realWorldImpact: API key/token theft from logging systems
Secrets leaked through log aggregation tools

remediation.after: logger.info(f"Login attempt for user: {username}")  # Only log username, not password
remediation.explanation: Never log passwords, tokens, API keys, credit cards, or other secrets. Log only non-sensitive identifiers like username, user ID, or transaction ID. Sanitize logs before storage.

attackVector.description: Sensitive data in logs can be exposed through log aggregation systems, backup files, or compromised logging servers, leading to credential theft and unauthorized access
attackVector.exploitExample: logger.info(f"User logged in with password: {password}")  # Password exposed in logs!
realWorldImpact: API key/token theft from logging systems
Secrets leaked through log aggregation tools

remediation.after: logger.info(f"Login attempt for user: {username}")  # Only log username, not password
remediation.explanation: Never log passwords, tokens, API keys, credit cards, or other secrets. Log only non-sensitive identifiers like username, user ID, or transaction ID. Sanitize logs before storage.

vulnerabilities.message: AI-generated code detected (MEDIUM confidence): 1 hallucinated method(s) found (.add)
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Replace hallucinated methods with correct Python equivalents. Found: .add(). Review and rewrite AI-generated code sections.

attackVector.description: AI-generated code with hallucinated methods creates runtime AttributeError exceptions exposing system internals through stack traces. Attackers can trigger these errors repeatedly to map application structure and identify vulnerable endpoints. Inconsistent code patterns (mixing camelCase and snake_case) make security reviews difficult, allowing real vulnerabilities to hide among AI-generated noise. Over-engineered error handling may leak sensitive information in except blocks.
attackVector.exploitExample:
User triggers: items.push(value)
Result: AttributeError: 'list' object has no attribute 'push'
Stack trace reveals: Internal file paths, function names, database schema hints
Attacker uses this to map application architecture and plan targeted attacks.
realWorldImpact: Runtime errors revealing sensitive stack traces with internal paths
Logic bugs in access control or validation code (AI-generated if statements)
Performance degradation from inefficient AI-generated loops and algorithms
Maintenance burden: Developers spend hours debugging AI hallucinations
Hidden security vulnerabilities masked by over-engineered code patterns

remediation.after: items.append() for lists  # Correct Python
remediation.explanation: AI code generators (like GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT, Claude) can hallucinate non-existent methods or generate over-engineered patterns. This creates reliability issues and potential security vulnerabilities. Hallucinated methods cause AttributeError exceptions that expose stack traces with sensitive information. Over-engineered code patterns make security audits difficult, hiding real vulnerabilities. Always verify AI-generated code matches Python specifications (PEP 8) and follows security best practices.

vulnerabilities.message: Missing await on potentially async function call
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add await keyword before async function calls

attackVector.description: Calling an async function without await causes the function to return a coroutine object instead of the expected result, leading to silent failures where code appears to run but does nothing.
attackVector.exploitExample: result = fetch_user_data()  # Returns coroutine, not user data

remediation.after: user = await get_user_by_id(user_id)
remediation.explanation: Always use await with async functions - missing await causes silent failures

vulnerabilities.message: Exception handling with continue/break may mask important errors
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Handle exceptions appropriately instead of using flow control statements

attackVector.description: Using continue/break in exception handlers can mask security-critical errors
realWorldImpact: Error accumulation leading to system instability
Missing security validations in batch operations

remediation.explanation: Flow control in exception handlers can hide important security errors that require attention

vulnerabilities.message: Path Traversal vulnerability - unrestricted file access
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use pathlib.Path.resolve() and validate against allowed base directory

attackVector.description: Path traversal allows attackers to access files outside intended directories using "../" sequences, potentially reading sensitive system files.
attackVector.exploitExample: open(user_path)  where user_path = "../../../etc/passwd"

remediation.after:
from pathlib import Path
safe_path = (Path(base_dir) / user_file).resolve()
if not safe_path.is_relative_to(base_dir):
    raise ValueError("Invalid path")
with open(safe_path) as f:
remediation.explanation: Always resolve paths and validate they stay within allowed base directory using Path.resolve() and is_relative_to()

vulnerabilities.triageReason: CVSS 7.5 (high) + Broken Access Control + production code

vulnerabilities.message: Resource may not be properly cleaned up in exception scenarios
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use with statements or try-finally blocks to ensure resource cleanup

attackVector.description: Improper resource cleanup during exceptions can lead to resource leaks and denial of service
attackVector.exploitExample: file = open("sensitive.txt"); data = file.read() # no finally or with statement
realWorldImpact: Memory leaks from unclosed file handles
Denial of service from resource exhaustion

remediation.after:
with open("data.txt") as file:
    # operations here
remediation.explanation: Resources opened without guaranteed cleanup can cause leaks when exceptions occur

vulnerabilities.message: Exception details may be exposed in HTTP responses
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Log detailed errors server-side, return generic messages to clients

attackVector.description: Exposing exception details reveals sensitive internal application information
attackVector.exploitExample: return jsonify({"error": str(e)}) # exposes full exception details to client
realWorldImpact: File paths and system information disclosure
Database schema and connection details revelation

remediation.after: logger.error("Operation failed", exc_info=True); return "Internal server error"
remediation.explanation: Exception details contain sensitive debugging information that should not be exposed to clients

vulnerabilities.message: Exception caught and silently ignored with pass statement
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Add proper error handling, logging, or re-raise the exception

attackVector.description: Silent exception suppression can hide security vulnerabilities and cause unpredictable behavior
attackVector.exploitExample:
except Exception:
    pass  # security errors completely ignored
realWorldImpact: Data integrity issues from ignored validation errors
Audit trail gaps from suppressed security events

remediation.explanation: Silent exception suppression prevents visibility into potentially security-critical failures

vulnerabilities.message: input() without validation can accept malicious data
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Validate and sanitize all user input with type conversion or regex

attackVector.description: Python's input() accepts any string, which can be manipulated to cause issues if not validated (especially in scripts with system access).
attackVector.exploitExample: filename = input("Enter filename: ")  # User enters "../../etc/passwd"
realWorldImpact: Command injection if passed to shell
Denial of Service with large inputs

remediation.explanation: Always validate and convert user input. Use int()/float() for numbers, regex for strings, and whitelist validation

vulnerabilities.message: print() can leak sensitive information
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Use logging module with appropriate levels
vulnerabilities.owasp: A09:2025 - Security Logging and Monitoring Failures

attackVector.description: print() statements can expose sensitive data in logs, stack traces, or console output accessible to unauthorized users.
attackVector.exploitExample: print(f"User logged in: {user.email}, token: {auth_token}")  # Exposes credentials

remediation.after:
import logging
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
logger.info(f"User logged in: {user.email}")  # No sensitive data
remediation.explanation: Use logging module with appropriate levels. Never log sensitive data (tokens, passwords, keys)

vulnerabilities.triageReason: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures + production code

vulnerabilities.message: Detailed error information may be exposed to users
vulnerabilities.suggestion: Ensure exc_info logs are server-side only and not exposed to users

attackVector.description: Exception info exposure reveals internal errors and stack traces

remediation.after: logging.exception("error")  # Includes traceback by default, server-side only
remediation.explanation: exc_info=True includes full stack traces in logs which may be exposed
